summary: African-American poet, novelist, and civil rights leader of the Harlem Renaissance. Known for the poem 'Lift Ev'ry Voice and Sing' (called the 'Black National Anthem'), the poetry collection God's Trombones ('Seven Negro Sermons in Verse'), and the novel The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man about racial passing. First Black leader of the NAACP.

works.name: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man
works.description: Johnson's 1912 novel, first published anonymously, about an unnamed biracial narrator who decides to 'pass' as white after witnessing a lynching. The narrator first learns he is biracial when his principal asks 'all of the white scholars' to stand. He plays ragtime piano for a rich white gentleman and travels to Europe, but after the lynching decides to marry a white woman and have white-passing children. The novel ends with the narrator wondering if he has 'traded his birthright for a mess of pottage.' His valedictorian classmate 'Shiny' becomes a professor at an HBCU. While in Paris, he sees his father and half-sister at a performance of Gounod's Faust. An argument about race occurs between 'the old soldier' and 'the Texan.'

clues.clue: Unnamed biracial narrator passes as white after witnessing a lynching
examples: the title character of this author's Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man decides to pass as white after witnessing a lynching
This author wrote about a narrator who is told his father has 'the best blood in the South' and attempts to pass as white

clues.clue: Ends with narrator wondering if he 'traded his birthright for a mess of pottage'
examples: This character ends the novel he appears in by stating he sold his birthright for a 'mess of pottage'

clues.clue: Narrator plays ragtime for a rich white gentleman; travels to Europe
examples: This leading figure in the NAACP wrote a novel about a talented pianist who travels to Europe playing for a rich white cosmopolitan man

clues.clue: First published anonymously in 1912; learns he is biracial when principal asks white scholars to stand
examples: this unnamed narrator of a novel first published anonymously in 1912. This character first learns he is biracial after his principal asks 'all of the white scholars' in his class to stand

clues.clue: Sees his father and half-sister at Gounod's Faust in Paris
examples: While in Paris, this character from a different novel sees his father and half-sister at a performance of Gounod's Faust

works.name: God's Trombones
works.description: A poetry collection subtitled 'Seven Negro Sermons in Verse,' illustrated by Aaron Douglas. Includes 'The Creation' (in which God says 'I'm lonely -- I'll make me a world'), 'Go Down, Death' (a funeral sermon about Sister Caroline being taken to heaven from her 'big bed of pain'), and 'The Prodigal Son' (warning 'your arm's too short to box with God'). The collection drew from the spiritual 'Go Down, Moses.'

clues.clue: 'The Creation': God says 'I'm lonely -- I'll make me a world'
examples: the author of a poem whose speaker declares 'I'm lonely -- / I'll make me a world'
Aaron Douglas illustrated one of his poetry collections, which includes a poem in which God says 'I'm lonely -- I'll make me a world'

clues.clue: 'Go Down, Death': funeral sermon; Sister Caroline taken to heaven from 'big bed of pain'
examples: Another of this author's poems describes how Sister Caroline is taken away from her 'big bed of pain' by the gentle title character

clues.clue: 'The Prodigal Son': 'your arm's too short to box with God'
examples: This author warned 'your arm's too short to box with God' at the beginning of a poem about a young man seduced by the pleasures of Babylon

clues.clue: 'Seven Negro Sermons in Verse' subtitle; illustrated by Aaron Douglas
examples: He included his poems 'Go Down Death,' 'The Prodigal Son,' and 'The Creation' in a book of 'Seven Negro Sermons in Verse'

clues.clue: Imagines 'the earth shall melt away... the sky will roll up like a scroll' and a voice crying 'Time shall be no more!'
examples: One of this author's poems imagines that 'the earth shall melt away and be dissolved and the sky will roll up like a scroll'

works.name: Lift Ev'ry Voice and Sing
works.description: Known as the 'Black National Anthem,' this poem/song asks 'let our rejoicing rise, high as the listening skies' and states 'yet with a steady beat, have not our weary feet come to the place for which our fathers sighed?' The first stanza ends 'let us march on till victory is won.' Maya Angelou's eighth-grade graduation class sang it in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings, making her realize she was 'a member of the proud, graduating, negro race.' The song instructs listeners to perform its title action 'Till earth and heaven ring, / Ring with the harmonies of Liberty.'

examples: This author of The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man also wrote the 'Black National Anthem' Lift Every Voice and Sing

clues.clue: 'Let our rejoicing rise, high as the listening skies'; 'weary feet come to the place for which our fathers sighed'
examples: he wrote 'yet with a steady beat, have not our weary feet come to the place for which our fathers sighed?'

clues.clue: Sung at Maya Angelou's eighth-grade graduation in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
examples: During a performance of this song at her eighth grade graduation, Angelou realized that she was... 'a member of the proud, graduating, negro race'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Johnson was the first Black leader of the NAACP. He was a central figure of the Harlem Renaissance. He served as consul to Venezuela. He tried to find a producer for his comic opera Tolosa. He also wrote a poem inspired by Moses, 'Let My People Go.'

clues.clue: First Black leader of the NAACP
examples: This first black leader of the NAACP and author of the poetry collection God's Trombones
give the surname of the Harlem Renaissance poet of God's Trombones
This author, who could not find a producer for his comic opera Tolosa, served as a consul to Venezuela

comprehensive_summary:
James Weldon Johnson is a central figure of the Harlem Renaissance and one of the most frequently clued African-American writers in quizbowl. His novel The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (1912, first published anonymously) follows an unnamed biracial narrator who decides to pass as white after witnessing a lynching. The narrator plays ragtime piano for a wealthy white patron and travels to Europe, but ultimately concludes by wondering if he has 'traded his birthright for a mess of pottage.' The novel is a key text in the literature of racial passing.

His poetry collection God's Trombones ('Seven Negro Sermons in Verse'), illustrated by Aaron Douglas, is frequently clued for individual poems: 'The Creation' features God saying 'I'm lonely -- I'll make me a world'; 'Go Down, Death' is a funeral sermon about Sister Caroline being taken to heaven; and 'The Prodigal Son' warns 'your arm's too short to box with God.'

'Lift Ev'ry Voice and Sing,' known as the 'Black National Anthem,' is perhaps Johnson's most famous work in popular culture. Maya Angelou's I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings describes her eighth-grade class singing it, making her realize she was 'a member of the proud, graduating, negro race.'

Johnson was the first Black leader of the NAACP and served as consul to Venezuela. He attempted to find a producer for his comic opera Tolosa.
